fact: Vesuvius had been quiet for roughly five hundred years before the 1631 eruption (last significant eruption around 1139)
source: Rolandi, G., Barrella, A.M., & Borrelli, A. 'The 1631 eruption of Vesuvius.' Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research, 58 (1993), pp. 183–201

fact: Residents had built homes on old lava flows they no longer recognized as volcanic material
source: Widely documented; discussed in Scarth, Alwyn. 'Vesuvius: A Biography' (2009), Princeton University Press

fact: Devastating lahars (mudflows) inundated towns during the 1631 eruption, filling doorways and streets in moments
source: Rosi, M., Principe, C., & Vecci, R. 'The 1631 Vesuvian eruption: A reconstruction based on historical and stratigraphical data.' Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research, 58 (1993), pp. 151–182

fact: The sea withdrew from the Bay of Naples during the eruption, pushed back by pyroclastic debris and volcanic material, before rushing back in
source: Scarth, Alwyn. 'Vesuvius: A Biography' (2009), Princeton University Press; also documented in contemporary accounts by Giuliani and other 17th-century observers

fact: Local churches and buildings were constructed using stone quarried from solidified lava of earlier eruptions
source: Widely documented; discussed in Lancaster, Lynne C. and others on Vesuvian building materials; also Scarth, 'Vesuvius: A Biography' (2009)

fact: Approximately four thousand people were killed in the 1631 eruption of Vesuvius
source: Rosi, Principe & Vecci (1993); death toll estimates range from ~3,000 to ~6,000, with ~4,000 being a commonly cited figure. See also the Osservatorio Vesuviano (INGV) historical records.
